The user wants me to research how the IC10 editor implements the cursor/typing window in the game. I need to look at the InputSourceCode.cs file and related files to understand how they handle text input, cursor/caret, and the editor implementation.

Let me search for and read the relevant files.I'll research how the IC10 editor implements its cursor and text input system. Let me examine the game's source code. Now let me read the EditorLineOfCode component to understand how individual lines handle their input and cursor: Now let me look for how the TMP_InputField is configured in the game - specifically its caret styling and selection: Let me look at the InputWindow to see how the game handles single-line input as well: Now let me look at how the game handles focus and multi-line text input in more detail. Let me also search for how the TextMeshPro Input Field is configured in the codebase: Let me look at Localization.ParseScript to understand the syntax highlighting system: This is very insightful! The IC10 editor has a dual-text system. Let me understand this architecture better: Now I understand the architecture! Let me summarize the key findings:

## IC10 Editor Architecture - Deep Research Summary

### Key Architecture Discovery

The IC10 editor uses a **dual-text overlay system** - this is the crucial insight:

1. **`TMP_InputField`** (`InputField`) - For actual text editing and cursor/caret
   - Contains the actual editable text (plain, unstyled)
   - Uses the built-in `TMP_InputField.caretPosition` for cursor tracking
   - The text is essentially invisible (same color as background or very transparent)

2. **`TextMeshProUGUI`** (`FormattedText`) - For syntax-highlighted display
   - Overlaid exactly on top of the input field
   - Shows the same text but with rich text color tags (syntax highlighting)
   - Read-only display layer

3. **`TextMeshProUGUI`** (`InputText`) - Plain text mirror
   - Used for layout sizing

### Cursor Implementation Details

From `InputSourceCode.cs`:

```csharp
// Caret position is tracked via the CurrentLine's InputField
private int CaretPosition
{
    get { return this.CurrentLine.InputField.caretPosition; }
    set { this.CurrentLine.InputField.caretPosition = value; }
}

// Previous caret position tracked for multi-line navigation
private int _previousCaretPosition;

// Key handling in HandleInput():
// - Home key sets caret to 0
// - End key sets caret to CurrentLine.Text.Length  
// - Up/Down arrows preserve _previousCaretPosition when switching lines
```

### Line Management

From `EditorLineOfCode.cs`:

```csharp
// Each line is a prefab with these components:
public TMP_InputField InputField;      // Actual input with cursor
public TextMeshProUGUI InputText;      // Plain text display
public TextMeshProUGUI FormattedText;  // Syntax-highlighted display
public TextMeshProUGUI LineNumber;     // "0.", "1.", etc.

// Clicking a line activates it:
public void OnPointerClick(PointerEventData eventData)
{
    EditorLineOfCode.CurrentLine = this;
}

// Setting CurrentLine auto-focuses the input field:
public static EditorLineOfCode CurrentLine
{
    set {
        _currentLine = value;
        _currentLine.InputField.ActivateInputField(); // <-- Key for cursor!
    }
}
```

### Critical Focus Management

```csharp
// Track if editor is focused for input handling
private bool _editorIsFocused;

void Update()
{
    if (InputState == InputPanelState.Waiting && _editorIsFocused)
    {
        HandleInput();  // Process keyboard navigation
    }
    _editorIsFocused = CurrentLine.InputField.isFocused;
}
```

### Your Current Issue

The Station Planner uses a single `TMP_InputField` - which should work, but you need to ensure:

1. **`ActivateInputField()`** is called when the editor panel receives focus
2. **`isFocused`** state is being checked properly
3. The input field has a proper **raycast target** (the background image needs `raycastTarget = true`)
4. **`customCaretColor = true`** and `caretColor` are set

Would you like me to update your Station Planner to ensure the cursor works properly based on these findings?

1. Need to call `ActivateInputField()` when the editor receives focus
2. Need to track `isFocused` state properly
3. Need proper raycast target on the input field background
4. Need `customCaretColor = true` and `caretColor` set

Looking at the current code, I already have:
- `customCaretColor = true`
- `caretColor = ACCENT`

What's missing:

2. Properly activating the input field when clicking on it

Let me implement these improvements based on the IC10 editor pattern.I'll implement the cursor improvements based on the IC10 editor research. The key changes are:

1. Call `ActivateInputField()` when showing window and selecting files
2. Add click-to-focus behavior on the editor panel
3. Track focus state properly
4. Add a focus handler component Now let me build to make sure everything compiles: Build succeeded. Here's what I implemented based on the IC10 editor research:

## Cursor Implementation Changes

**1. Focus State Tracking** (like IC10's `_editorIsFocused`)
- Added `_editorIsFocused` field to track if the input field is focused
- `UpdateWindow()` now checks `_editorInputField.isFocused` each frame

**2. ActivateInputField() Calls** (like IC10's `CurrentLine` setter)
- `Show()` now calls `ActivateInputField()` and `Select()` when opening the window
- `SelectFile()` now activates the input field after loading content

**3. EditorClickHandler Component** (like IC10's `EditorLineOfCode`)
- New component that implements `IPointerClickHandler` and `IPointerDownHandler`
- Calls `ActivateInputField()` when clicking anywhere in the editor area
- Attached to the input field GameObject

The key insight from the IC10 editor is that `TMP_InputField.ActivateInputField()` must be called to show the cursor - it doesn't automatically activate just from having focus. The IC10 editor does this on every line click via `OnPointerClick` and `OnPointerDown`.

Test it with F6 hot-reload - clicking in the editor area should now show the blinking orange cursor.

The user wants to know what sprite assets the IC10 editor uses to build its window. I need to search the Assembly-CSharp code for references to sprites, images, or UI assets used by the InputSourceCode (IC10 editor) class.Let me look more specifically at the InputSourceCode fields and what UI references it has: The IC10 editor uses Unity prefabs in the scene that are set up in the Unity Editor. The sprite assets aren't loaded by code - they're assigned to the `TranslucentImage` component in the Unity scene. Let me search for any sprite references or asset paths: Let me look at how Stationpedia defines its sprites/images: Based on my research, here's what I found about the **IC10 editor's UI assets**:

## IC10 Editor Window Components (InputSourceCode.cs)

The IC10 editor doesn't load sprites by name at runtime - it uses **Unity prefabs** with serialized references set in the Unity Editor. The key UI components are:

### Main UI References
| Field | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `Background` | `TranslucentImage` | Main window background (blur effect) |
| `BackGroundFadeImage` | `Image` | Full-screen fade/dim behind the window |
| `CodeInputWindow` | `UiComponentRenderer` | The main code editor panel container |
| `Window` | `RectTransform` | Window rect for sizing |
| `LineOfCodePrefab` | `EditorLineOfCode` | Prefab for each line of code |
| `LineParent` | `RectTransform` | Parent for line prefabs |

### TranslucentImage Properties
The `TranslucentImage` component (from LeTai asset) controls the semi-transparent background:
- `spriteBlending = 0.65f` (65% sprite, 35% blur when not opaque)
- `spriteBlending = 1f` (100% solid when opaque)
- Uses shader `"UI/TranslucentImage"`

### The Sprite Assets
Since these are **serialized Unity prefab references**, the actual sprite names would be in the Unity scene/prefab files (`.unity` or `.prefab`), not in the C# code. The game doesn't use `Resources.Load()` for UI sprites.

**The window styling comes from:**
1. `TranslucentImage` component for blur effect
2. `Outline` component for borders (uses color, not sprite)
3. Colors set via code (like the orange `#FBB03B` we found earlier)

For your Station Planner, you're building the UI entirely in code, which is correct since you don't have access to modify the game's Unity assets. Your current approach of using `Image` + `Outline` components with color styling is the right way to match the look without accessing private sprites.

```json
  "content": "",
  "textEditContent": "using System.Collections.Generic;\nusing System.Text;\nusing UnityEngine;\nusing UnityEngine.UI;\nusing UnityEngine.EventSystems;\nusing TMPro;\nusing Assets.Scripts.UI;\n\nnamespace StationpediaAscended.Debug\n{\n    /// &lt;summary&gt;\n    /// Debug tool to inspect UI assets under the mouse cursor.\n    /// Toggle with console command: assetdisplay\n    /// &lt;/summary&gt;\n    public class UIAssetInspector : MonoBehaviour\n    {\n        public static UIAssetInspector Instance { get; private set; }\n        public static bool IsEnabled { get; private set; } = false;\n        \n        private Canvas _tooltipCanvas;\n        private GameObject _tooltipPanel;\n        private TextMeshProUGUI _tooltipText;\n        private RectTransform _tooltipRect;\n        \n        private PointerEventData _pointerData;\n        private List&lt;RaycastResult&gt; _raycastResults = new List&lt;RaycastResult&gt;();\n        private StringBuilder _sb = new StringBuilder();\n        \n        // Colors for the tooltip\n        private static readonly Color BG_COLOR = new Color(0.05f, 0.05f, 0.1f, 0.95f);\n        private static readonly Color BORDER_COLOR = new Color(0f, 1f, 0.5f, 1f); // Green for debug\n        private static readonly Color TEXT_COLOR = new Color(0.9f, 1f, 0.9f, 1f);\n        \n        public static void Initialize()\n        {\n            if (Instance != null) return;\n            \n            var go = new GameObject(\"UIAssetInspector\");\n            Instance = go.AddComponent&lt;UIAssetInspector&gt;();\n            DontDestroyOnLoad(go);\n            \n            Instance.CreateTooltip();\n            Instance.SetEnabled(false);\n            \n            UnityEngine.Debug.Log(\"[UIAssetInspector] Initialized. Use 'assetdisplay' command to toggle.\");\n        }\n        \n        public static void Toggle()\n        {\n            if (Instance == null) Initialize();\n            Instance.SetEnabled(!IsEnabled);\n        }\n        \n        public static void Cleanup()\n        {\n            if (Instance != null)\n            {\n                if (Instance._tooltipCanvas != null)\n                    Destroy(Instance._tooltipCanvas.gameObject);\n                Destroy(Instance.gameObject);\n                Instance = null;\n            }\n        }\n        \n        private void SetEnabled(bool enabled)\n        {\n            IsEnabled = enabled;\n            if (_tooltipCanvas != null)\n                _tooltipCanvas.enabled = enabled;\n                \n            UnityEngine.Debug.Log($\"[UIAssetInspector] {(enabled ? \"ENABLED\" : \"DISABLED\")}\");\n        }\n        \n        private void CreateTooltip()\n        {\n            // Create canvas for tooltip (highest sort order to always be on top)\n            var canvasGO = new GameObject(\"AssetInspectorCanvas\");\n            canvasGO.transform.SetParent(transform);\n            \n            _tooltipCanvas = canvasGO.AddComponent&lt;Canvas&gt;();\n            _tooltipCanvas.renderMode = RenderMode.ScreenSpaceOverlay;\n            _tooltipCanvas.sortingOrder = 32000; // Very high to be on top of everything\n            \n            var scaler = canvasGO.AddComponent&lt;CanvasScaler&gt;();\n            scaler.uiScaleMode = CanvasScaler.ScaleMode.ScaleWithScreenSize;\n            scaler.referenceResolution = new Vector2(1920, 1080);\n            \n            // Don't add GraphicRaycaster - we don't want this to block raycasts\n            \n            // Create tooltip panel\n            _tooltipPanel = new GameObject(\"TooltipPanel\");\n            _tooltipPanel.transform.SetParent(canvasGO.transform, false);\n            \n            _tooltipRect = _tooltipPanel.AddComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\n            _tooltipRect.pivot = new Vector2(0, 1); // Top-left pivot\n            _tooltipRect.anchorMin = new Vector2(0, 0);\n            _tooltipRect.anchorMax = new Vector2(0, 0);\n            \n            var bg = _tooltipPanel.AddComponent&lt;Image&gt;();\n            bg.color = BG_COLOR;\n            bg.raycastTarget = false;\n            \n            var outline = _tooltipPanel.AddComponent&lt;Outline&gt;();\n            outline.effectColor = BORDER_COLOR;\n            outline.effectDistance = new Vector2(2, 2);\n            \n            // Content size fitter\n            var csf = _tooltipPanel.AddComponent&lt;ContentSizeFitter&gt;();\n            csf.horizontalFit = ContentSizeFitter.FitMode.PreferredSize;\n            csf.verticalFit = ContentSizeFitter.FitMode.PreferredSize;\n            \n            var vlg = _tooltipPanel.AddComponent&lt;VerticalLayoutGroup&gt;();\n            vlg.padding = new RectOffset(10, 10, 8, 8);\n            vlg.childAlignment = TextAnchor.UpperLeft;\n            vlg.childControlWidth = true;\n            vlg.childControlHeight = true;\n            \n            // Text\n            var textGO = new GameObject(\"Text\");\n            textGO.transform.SetParent(_tooltipPanel.transform, false);\n            \n            _tooltipText = textGO.AddComponent&lt;TextMeshProUGUI&gt;();\n            _tooltipText.fontSize = 12;\n            _tooltipText.color = TEXT_COLOR;\n            _tooltipText.alignment = TextAlignmentOptions.TopLeft;\n            _tooltipText.enableWordWrapping = false;\n            _tooltipText.overflowMode = TextOverflowModes.Overflow;\n            _tooltipText.raycastTarget = false;\n            _tooltipText.richText = true;\n            \n            var textLE = textGO.AddComponent&lt;LayoutElement&gt;();\n            textLE.minWidth = 200;\n            textLE.minHeight = 50;\n        }\n        \n        void Update()\n        {\n            if (!IsEnabled || _tooltipCanvas == null) return;\n            \n            // Position tooltip near mouse\n            Vector2 mousePos = Input.mousePosition;\n            _tooltipRect.position = mousePos + new Vector2(20, -20);\n            \n            // Clamp to screen\n            Vector2 size = _tooltipRect.sizeDelta;\n            float maxX = Screen.width - size.x - 10;\n            float minY = size.y + 10;\n            \n            if (_tooltipRect.position.x &gt; maxX)\n                _tooltipRect.position = new Vector2(mousePos.x - size.x - 20, _tooltipRect.position.y);\n            if (_tooltipRect.position.y &lt; minY)\n                _tooltipRect.position = new Vector2(_tooltipRect.position.x, mousePos.y + size.y + 20);\n            \n            // Raycast to find UI elements\n            UpdateTooltipContent();\n        }\n        \n        private void UpdateTooltipContent()\n        {\n            _sb.Clear();\n            _sb.AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#00FF80&gt;&lt;b&gt;UI ASSET INSPECTOR&lt;/b&gt;&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            _sb.AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#808080&gt;─────────────────────&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            \n            // Get all EventSystems and raycast\n            var eventSystem = EventSystem.current;\n            if (eventSystem == null)\n            {\n                _sb.AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#FF8080&gt;No EventSystem found&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                _tooltipText.text = _sb.ToString();\n                return;\n            }\n            \n            if (_pointerData == null)\n                _pointerData = new PointerEventData(eventSystem);\n            \n            _pointerData.position = Input.mousePosition;\n            _raycastResults.Clear();\n            \n            // Raycast all\n            eventSystem.RaycastAll(_pointerData, _raycastResults);\n            \n            if (_raycastResults.Count == 0)\n            {\n                _sb.AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#FFFF80&gt;No UI elements under cursor&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            }\n            else\n            {\n                _sb.AppendLine($\"&lt;color=#80FFFF&gt;Found {_raycastResults.Count} element(s):&lt;/color&gt;\\n\");\n                \n                int count = 0;\n                foreach (var result in _raycastResults)\n                {\n                    if (count &gt;= 8) // Limit to prevent huge tooltips\n                    {\n                        _sb.AppendLine($\"&lt;color=#808080&gt;... and {_raycastResults.Count - count} more&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                        break;\n                    }\n                    \n                    var go = result.gameObject;\n                    if (go == null) continue;\n                    \n                    // Skip our own inspector tooltip\n                    if (go.transform.IsChildOf(transform)) continue;\n                    \n                    AppendGameObjectInfo(go, count + 1);\n                    count++;\n                }\n            }\n            \n            _tooltipText.text = _sb.ToString();\n            LayoutRebuilder.ForceRebuildLayoutImmediate(_tooltipRect);\n        }\n        \n        private void AppendGameObjectInfo(GameObject go, int index)\n        {\n            _sb.AppendLine($\"&lt;color=#FFD700&gt;[{index}] {go.name}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            \n            // Get hierarchy path (up to 3 levels)\n            string path = GetHierarchyPath(go.transform, 3);\n            _sb.AppendLine($\"  &lt;color=#A0A0A0&gt;Path: {path}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            \n            // Check for Image component and sprite\n            var image = go.GetComponent&lt;Image&gt;();\n            if (image != null)\n            {\n                _sb.Append(\"  &lt;color=#80FF80&gt;Image:&lt;/color&gt; \");\n                if (image.sprite != null)\n                {\n                    _sb.AppendLine($\"&lt;color=#FFFFFF&gt;{image.sprite.name}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                    if (image.sprite.texture != null)\n                        _sb.AppendLine($\"    &lt;color=#808080&gt;Texture: {image.sprite.texture.name}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                }\n                else\n                {\n                    _sb.AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#808080&gt;(no sprite)&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                }\n                _sb.AppendLine($\"    &lt;color=#808080&gt;Color: {ColorToHex(image.color)}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                \n                // Check if it's a TranslucentImage\n                var translucentImage = go.GetComponent&lt;LeTai.Asset.TranslucentImage.TranslucentImage&gt;();\n                if (translucentImage != null)\n                {\n                    _sb.AppendLine($\"    &lt;color=#FF80FF&gt;TranslucentImage: spriteBlending={translucentImage.spriteBlending:F2}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                }\n            }\n            \n            // Check for RawImage\n            var rawImage = go.GetComponent&lt;RawImage&gt;();\n            if (rawImage != null)\n            {\n                _sb.Append(\"  &lt;color=#80FF80&gt;RawImage:&lt;/color&gt; \");\n                if (rawImage.texture != null)\n                    _sb.AppendLine($\"&lt;color=#FFFFFF&gt;{rawImage.texture.name}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                else\n                    _sb.AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#808080&gt;(no texture)&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            }\n            \n            // Check for TextMeshProUGUI\n            var tmp = go.GetComponent&lt;TextMeshProUGUI&gt;();\n            if (tmp != null)\n            {\n                string textPreview = tmp.text;\n                if (textPreview.Length &gt; 30)\n                    textPreview = textPreview.Substring(0, 30) + \"...\";\n                textPreview = textPreview.Replace(\"\\n\", \"\\\\n\").Replace(\"&lt;\", \"〈\").Replace(\"&gt;\", \"〉\");\n                \n                _sb.AppendLine($\"  &lt;color=#80FFFF&gt;TMP:&lt;/color&gt; \\\"{textPreview}\\\"\");\n                if (tmp.font != null)\n                    _sb.AppendLine($\"    &lt;color=#808080&gt;Font: {tmp.font.name}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            }\n            \n            // Check for Button\n            var button = go.GetComponent&lt;Button&gt;();\n            if (button != null)\n            {\n                _sb.AppendLine($\"  &lt;color=#FFFF80&gt;Button&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            }\n            \n            // Check for UiComponentRenderer\n            var uiRenderer = go.GetComponent&lt;UiComponentRenderer&gt;();\n            if (uiRenderer != null)\n            {\n                _sb.AppendLine($\"  &lt;color=#FF8080&gt;UiComponentRenderer&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            }\n            \n            // Check for Canvas\n            var canvas = go.GetComponent&lt;Canvas&gt;();\n            if (canvas != null)\n            {\n                _sb.AppendLine($\"  &lt;color=#8080FF&gt;Canvas: sortOrder={canvas.sortingOrder}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n            }\n            \n            // List other notable components\n            var components = go.GetComponents&lt;Component&gt;();\n            foreach (var comp in components)\n            {\n                if (comp == null) continue;\n                string typeName = comp.GetType().Name;\n                \n                // Skip common/already listed components\n                if (typeName == \"RectTransform\" || typeName == \"CanvasRenderer\" || \n                    typeName == \"Image\" || typeName == \"RawImage\" || typeName == \"Button\" ||\n                    typeName == \"TextMeshProUGUI\" || typeName == \"Canvas\" || \n                    typeName == \"UiComponentRenderer\" || typeName == \"TranslucentImage\")\n                    continue;\n                \n                // Show interesting components\n                if (typeName.Contains(\"Scroll\") || typeName.Contains(\"Layout\") || \n                    typeName.Contains(\"Mask\") || typeName.Contains(\"Input\") ||\n                    typeName.Contains(\"Toggle\") || typeName.Contains(\"Slider\") ||\n                    typeName.Contains(\"Dropdown\") || typeName.Contains(\"Editor\"))\n                {\n                    _sb.AppendLine($\"  &lt;color=#C0C0C0&gt;{typeName}&lt;/color&gt;\");\n                }\n            }\n            \n            _sb.AppendLine();\n        }\n        \n        private string GetHierarchyPath(Transform t, int maxDepth)\n        {\n            if (t == null || maxDepth &lt;= 0) return \"\";\n            \n            var parts = new List&lt;string&gt;();\n            var current = t;\n            int depth = 0;\n            \n            while (current != null &amp;&amp; depth &lt; maxDepth)\n            {\n                parts.Insert(0, current.name);\n                current = current.parent;\n                depth++;\n            }\n            \n            if (current != null)\n                parts.Insert(0, \"...\");\n            \n            return string.Join(\"/\", parts);\n        }\n        \n        private string ColorToHex(Color c)\n        {\n            return $\"#{ColorUtility.ToHtmlStringRGBA(c)}\";\n        }\n    }\n}\n",
  "toolCallResult": null
}
```
Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

The user wants a debug tool that will show the name of any Unity UI asset under the mouse cursor. This is a great idea for reverse-engineering the game's UI assets. I'll create a new command handler that enables a mode where hovering over UI elements shows their names in a tooltip.

I'll need to:
1. Create a new debug utility class that can be toggled with a console command
2. Use raycasting to find UI elements under the mouse
3. Display a tooltip showing the GameObject name, component types, and any Image sprite names

Let me create this as a separate utility file.Great idea! I'll create a debug tool that shows the UI asset names under your mouse cursor. Now let me add the console command handler to the main plugin file: Let me continue reading the file to find where to add the console command, and check for existing patterns: I can see the pattern for adding console commands in the `RegisterConsoleCommands` method around line 1378. Now I need to:

1. First check that the UIAssetInspector.cs file exists
2. Add the `using` statement for the Debug namespace
3. Add the console command registration and initialize the UIAssetInspector Good, the file exists. Now let me add the console command registration and initialization: Now let me add the initialization of UIAssetInspector and the console command. First, I'll add the initialization in the Initialize method: Now let me add the console command registration: Now I need to add the command handler method. Let me add it after the `CurrentPageKeyCommand` method: Now I need to add cleanup in OnDestroy and also add an `IsEnabled` property to the UIAssetInspector. Let me first check the UIAssetInspector to add the property: The `IsEnabled` property already exists. Now let me add the cleanup call in the OnDestroy method: Now let me build the project to verify everything compiles correctly: There's a namespace conflict. The `Debug` namespace in `StationpediaAscended.Debug` is conflicting with `System.Diagnostics.Debug` references. Let me check the StationPlannerWindow.cs to see what's happening: The issue is that `Debug.Log` in `StationPlannerWindow.cs` now resolves to `StationpediaAscended.Debug.Log` instead of `UnityEngine.Debug.Log` because of the namespace. I need to rename the debug namespace to something that won't conflict. Let me rename it to `Diagnostics`: Now let's rebuild: The build succeeded. The `assetdisplay` console command is now ready. 

**Summary of what was added:**

1. **`UIAssetInspector.cs`** - A debug tool in `StationpediaAscended.Diagnostics` namespace that:
   - Creates a tooltip canvas that follows the mouse
   - Raycasts through all UI elements under the cursor
   - Displays for each element:
     - GameObject name
     - Hierarchy path (up to 3 levels)
     - **Image sprite name and texture name**
     - Image colors (as hex)
     - **TranslucentImage spriteBlending value** (for blur effects)
     - TextMeshProUGUI text preview and font name
     - Button, Canvas sortOrder, and other components

2. **Console command**: `assetdisplay` - toggles the inspector on/off

**To use:**
1. In-game, open the console (~)
2. Type `assetdisplay` and press Enter
3. Hover over UI elements to see their asset names
4. Type `assetdisplay` again to turn it off

This will let you discover the exact sprite names, textures, and TranslucentImage settings used by the IC10 editor so you can recreate its appearance in Station Planner.

The user is saying the console command "assetdisplay" is not being recognized. Let me check the RegisterConsoleCommands method to see if there's an issue with how the command is being registered.

The issue might be that the command registration is checking if "stationpediacenter" already exists before registering ANY commands, including the new one. Let me look at the code again.Let me check the console command registration logic: I see the problem! The early return check only looks for "stationpediacenter" - if that command exists, it returns early and skips registering ALL the other commands including "assetdisplay". This is a problem during hot-reload because the old commands exist but the new one doesn't.

Let me fix this by checking each command individually: Fixed! The issue was that the old code had a single early-return check that prevented ALL new commands from being registered if "stationpediacenter" already existed.

Now each command is checked individually before registration. After F6 reload, the `assetdisplay` command should now be available.

The user is saying that after hot reload (F6), the UI Asset Inspector tooltip is still showing green colors instead of the new orange/dark blue colors. This is likely because:

1. The UIAssetInspector is using `DontDestroyOnLoad` and the old instance persists across hot reloads
2. The `Initialize()` method checks `if (Instance != null) return;` - so it won't recreate with new colors

The issue is that the old instance with the old colors persists. We need to either:
1. Force cleanup the old instance on hot reload
2. Or the user needs to restart the game entirely

Let me check if there's a way to force the cleanup. The `Cleanup()` method exists but it might not be getting called during hot reload properly.

Actually, the simplest fix is to make the `Initialize()` method destroy any existing instance first, or to call `Cleanup()` before `Initialize()` in the main plugin's initialization.

Let me check the main plugin to see how it handles hot reload cleanup.The hot reload isn't cleaning up the old UIAssetInspector instance because it uses `DontDestroyOnLoad`. Let me fix the Initialize method to destroy the old instance and recreate with new settings: Now hot reload will destroy and recreate the inspector with the new colors. After F6 reload, the tooltip should now be dark blue with orange border.

!result.gameObject.transform.IsChildOf(transform))\r\n                    filteredResults.Add(result);\r\n            }\r\n            \r\n            // Header in first column\r\n            _columnBuilders[0].AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#FBB03B&gt;&lt;b&gt;== UI ASSET INSPECTOR ==&lt;/b&gt;&lt;/color&gt;\");\r\n            _columnBuilders[0].AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#606060&gt;------------------------&lt;/color&gt;\");\r\n            \r\n            if (filteredResults.Count == 0)\r\n            {\r\n                _columnBuilders[0].AppendLine(\"&lt;color=#FFFF80&gt;No UI elements under cursor&lt;/color&gt;\");\r\n                ApplyColumnsToUI(1);\r\n                return;\r\n            }\r\n            \r\n            _columnBuilders[0].AppendLine($\"&lt;color=#80FFFF&gt;Found {filteredResults.Count} element(s):&lt;/color&gt;\\n\");\r\n            \r\n            // Calculate how many columns we need\r\n            int totalItems = Mathf.Min(filteredResults.Count, ITEMS_PER_COLUMN * MAX_COLUMNS);\r\n            int columnsNeeded = Mathf.CeilToInt((float)totalItems / ITEMS_PER_COLUMN);\r\n            columnsNeeded = Mathf.Max(1, Mathf.Min(columnsNeeded, MAX_COLUMNS));\r\n            \r\n            // Distribute items across columns\r\n            int itemIndex = 0;\r\n            for (int col = 0; col &lt; columnsNeeded &amp;&amp; itemIndex &lt; totalItems; col++)\r\n            {\r\n                // First column already has header, so add fewer items\r\n                int itemsInThisColumn = (col == 0) ? ITEMS_PER_COLUMN - 1 : ITEMS_PER_COLUMN;\r\n                \r\n                for (int i = 0; i &lt; itemsInThisColumn &amp;&amp; itemIndex &lt; totalItems; i++)\r\n                {\r\n                    var go = filteredResults[itemIndex].gameObject;\r\n                    AppendGameObjectInfo(_columnBuilders[col], go, itemIndex + 1);\r\n                    itemIndex++;\r\n                }\r\n            }\r\n            \r\n            // Show remaining count if truncated\r\n            if (filteredResults.Count &gt; totalItems)\r\n            {\r\n                int lastCol = columnsNeeded - 1;\r\n                _columnBuilders[lastCol].AppendLine($\"&lt;color=#808080&gt;... and {filteredResults.Count - totalItems} more&lt;/color&gt;\");\r\n            }\r\n            \r\n            ApplyColumnsToUI(columnsNeeded);\r\n        }\r\n        \r\n        private void ApplyColumnsToUI(int columnsNeeded)\r\n        {\r\n            for (int i = 0; i &lt; _columnTexts.Count; i++)\r\n            {\r\n                bool shouldShow = i &lt; columnsNeeded;\r\n                _columnTexts[i].gameObject.SetActive(shouldShow);\r\n                if (shouldShow)\r\n                {\r\n                    _columnTexts[i].text = _columnBuilders[i].ToString();\r\n                }\r\n            }\r\n            LayoutRebuilder.ForceRebuildLayoutImmediate(_tooltipRect);\r\n        }\r\n        \r\n        private void AppendGameObjectInfo(StringBuilder sb, \n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n",

---

The user wants to show more elements by using multiple columns instead of truncating at 8 elements. I need to modify the UIAssetInspector to use a horizontal layout with multiple columns when there are many elements.I'll restructure the tooltip to use multiple columns with a horizontal layout: Now let me update the `UpdateTooltipContent` method to distribute elements across columns: Now update the `AppendGameObjectInfo` method to use the passed StringBuilder: Build succeeded. Now the tooltip will:
- Show up to **6 items per column**
- Use up to **4 columns** (total 24 items max)
- Automatically expand horizontally when there are more elements
- Display dark blue background with orange border (after F6 reload destroys the old instance)

---

_**User (2026-01-04 21:03Z)**_

Ok so using the asset reading tool we just made I can see that the ic10 editor is created using a couple different sprites that are then colored and given different opacity levels! 

Take a look at the screenshot. If I give you all these assets in a folder can you use them to recreate this?

---

_**Agent (model copilot/claude-opus-4.5)**_

The user is showing me the UI Asset Inspector output from the IC10 editor. I can see the key components:

1. **dialog-outline** - The orange border outline
   - Texture: dialog-outline
   - Color: #FE5B16FF (orange)

2. **dialog-bg** - The main background with blur
   - Texture: dialog-bg
   - Color: #007A4FFF (but wait, that's greenish - they're probably using tint)
   - **TranslucentImage: spriteBlending=1.00** - fully opaque sprite (no blur showing through)

3. **window-shadow** - Shadow effect
   - Texture: windows-shadow
   - Color: #FFFFFF80 (white with 50% opacity)
   - Has UiComponentRenderer

4. **PanelInputCode** - The main canvas container

toolbar.AddComponent&lt;HorizontalLayoutGroup&gt;();\r\n            hlg.spacing = 4;\r\n            hlg.padding = new RectOffset(6, 6, 3, 3);\r\n            hlg.childAlignment = TextAnchor.MiddleLeft;\r\n            hlg.childControlWidth = false;\r\n            hlg.childControlHeight = true;\r\n            hlg.childForceExpandWidth = false;\r\n            \r\n            // File buttons - simple text, no unicode\r\n            var newFileBtn = CreateToolbarBtn(toolbar.transform, \"New\", 45);\r\n            newFileBtn.onClick.AddListener(OnNewFile);\r\n            AddSimpleTooltip(newFileBtn.gameObject, \"Create new file\");\r\n            \r\n            var newFolderBtn = CreateToolbarBtn(toolbar.transform, \"Folder\", 55);\r\n            newFolderBtn.onClick.AddListener(OnNewFolder);\r\n            AddSimpleTooltip(newFolderBtn.gameObject, \"Create new folder\");\r\n            \r\n            var renameBtn = CreateToolbarBtn(toolbar.transform, \"Rename\", 60);\r\n            renameBtn.onClick.AddListener(OnRename);\r\n            AddSimpleTooltip(renameBtn.gameObject, \"Rename selected\");\r\n            \r\n            var deleteBtn = CreateToolbarBtn(toolbar.transform, \"Delete\", 55);\r\n            deleteBtn.onClick.AddListener(OnDelete);\r\n            AddSimpleTooltip(deleteBtn.gameObject, \"Delete selected\");\r\n            \r\n            var saveBtn = CreateToolbarBtn(toolbar.transform, \"Save\", 45);\r\n            saveBtn.onClick.AddListener(() =&gt; { SaveCurrentFile(); SaveFileSystem(); });\r\n            AddSimpleTooltip(saveBtn.gameObject, \"Save (Ctrl+S)\");\r\n            \r\n            // Separator\r\n            CreateSeparator(toolbar.transform);\r\n            \r\n            // Formatting buttons\r\n            var boldBtn = CreateToolbarBtn(toolbar.transform, \"B\", 26);\r\n            boldBtn.GetComponentInChildren&lt;TextMeshProUGUI&gt;().fontStyle = FontStyles.Bold;\r\n            boldBtn.onClick.AddListener(() =&gt; InsertTag(\"&lt;b&gt;\", \"&lt;/b&gt;\"));\r\n            AddSimpleTooltip(boldBtn.gameObject, \"Bold\");\r\n            \r\n \r\n        /// &lt;summary&gt;\r\n        /// Creates an IC10-style scrollbar with custom sprites\r\n        /// &lt;/summary&gt;\r\n        private void CreateIC10Scrollbar(Transform parent, ScrollRect scrollRect)\r\n        {\r\n            // Scrollbar container\r\n            var scrollbarGO = new GameObject(\"Scrollbar\");\r\n            scrollbarGO.transform.SetParent(parent, false);\r\n            var scrollbarRect = scrollbarGO.AddComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n            scrollbarRect.anchorMin = new Vector2(1, 0);\r\n            scrollbarRect.anchorMax = new Vector2(1, 1);\r\n            scrollbarRect.pivot = new Vector2(1, 0.5f);\r\n            scrollbarRect.anchoredPosition = Vector2.zero;\r\n            scrollbarRect.sizeDelta = new Vector2(12, 0);\r\n            \r\n            // Scrollbar background - IC10 uses scrollbar-bg sprite\r\n            var scrollbarBg = scrollbarGO.AddComponent&lt;Image&gt;();\r\n            var scrollbarBgSprite = LoadSlicedSprite(\"scrollbar-bg.png\", 4);\r\n            if (scrollbarBgSprite != null)\r\n            {\r\n                scrollbarBg.sprite = scrollbarBgSprite;\r\n                scrollbarBg.type = Image.Type.Sliced;\r\n            }\r\n            scrollbarBg.color = Color.white; // #FFFFFFFF as shown in inspector\r\n            \r\n            // Sliding area\r\n            var slidingAreaGO = new GameObject(\"Sliding Area\");\r\n            slidingAreaGO.transform.SetParent(scrollbarGO.transform, false);\r\n            var slidingRect = slidingAreaGO.AddComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n            slidingRect.anchorMin = Vector2.zero;\r\n            slidingRect.anchorMax = Vector2.one;\r\n            slidingRect.offsetMin = new Vector2(2, 2);\r\n            slidingRect.offsetMax = new Vector2(-2, -2);\r\n            \r\n            // Handle\r\n            var handleGO = new GameObject(\"Handle\");\r\n            handleGO.transform.SetParent(slidingAreaGO.transform, false);\r\n            var handleRect = handleGO.AddComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n            handleRect.anchorMin = Vector2.zero;\r\n uses scrollbar-handle sprite\r\n            var handleImg = handleGO.AddComponent&lt;Image&gt;();\r\n            var handleSprite = LoadSlicedSprite(\"scrollbar-handle.png\", 4);\r\n            if (handleSprite != null)\r\n            {\r\n                handleImg.sprite = handleSprite;\r\n                handleImg.type = Image.Type.Sliced;\r\n            }\r\n            handleImg.color = Color.white; // #FFFFFFFF\r\n            \r\n            // Scrollbar component\r\n            var scrollbar = scrollbarGO.AddComponent&lt;Scrollbar&gt;();\r\n            scrollbar.handleRect = handleRect;\r\n            scrollbar.direction = Scrollbar.Direction.BottomToTop;\r\n            scrollbar.targetGraphic = handleImg;\r\n            \r\n            // Set button colors for handle\r\n colors.pressedColor = new Color(0.6f, 0.6f, 0.6f, 1f);\r\n            scrollbar.colors = colors;\r\n            \r\n            // Connect to scroll rect\r\n            scrollRect.verticalScrollbar = scrollbar;\r\n            scrollRect.verticalScrollbarVisibility = ScrollRect.ScrollbarVisibility.AutoHideAndExpandViewport;\r\n            scrollRect.verticalScrollbarSpacing = 2",

The user wants me to research how the IC10 editor works (cursor, text input, line rendering) and then recreate the Station Planner text editor to match the visual style using the sprites they've provided.

Let me first research the IC10 editor code to understand:
1. How the cursor/caret works
2. How lines of code are rendered
3. The overall input mechanism

Then I'll update the Station Planner window to:
1. Use the same visual sprites (dialog-bg, dialog-outline, window-shadow, scrollbar-bg, scrollbar-handle)
2. Implement a proper text editor with blinking cursor
3. Match the layered structure

Let me start by finding the relevant code files.Let me research the IC10 editor code to understand how the cursor and text input works: Now let me check the images folder for available sprites: All the sprites are there. Now let me read the current StationPlannerWindow to understand its structure: Now I have a complete understanding. The IC10 editor uses:

1. **Layered sprites**: window-shadow → dialog-bg (TranslucentImage) → dialog-outline
2. **TMP_InputField** for each line with `InputText`, `FormattedText`, and built-in caret
3. The caret is built into TMP_InputField (orange, blinking)

For our note-taking use case, we can use a **single multi-line TMP_InputField** (which is simpler than the IC10 per-line approach), but styled with the same sprite layers. Let me rewrite the editor panel to match the IC10 visual style exactly: Now let me replace the CreateWindow method to use the IC10 sprite layering: Now let me add a new method that creates the main content area with file tree and editor, and update the editor panel to use IC10 styling: Now let me update the file tree panel and editor panel to use the new positioning and IC10 styling: Now let's build and test: Build succeeded. The Station Planner window has been rebuilt with IC10 editor-style UI:

**Key changes:**
1. **Layered sprite backgrounds** (like IC10):
   - `window-shadow.png` - Shadow layer (white @ 50% opacity)
   - `dialog-bg.png` - Main background using `TranslucentImage` with `spriteBlending=1.0`
   - `dialog-outline.png` - Orange border overlay (#FF5A16)

2. **Editor panel** (like PanelInputCode):
   - Inner black background @ 75% opacity (#000000BF)
   - IC10-style scrollbar using `scrollbar-bg.png` and `scrollbar-handle.png`
   - Orange blinking caret (via TMP_InputField built-in caret)
   - Proper scroll support for long notes

3. **Structure**:
   - File tree on left with semi-transparent dark overlay
   - Editor with scroll view and IC10-style scrollbar on right
   - All using the game's sprites from your images folder

After F6 reload, the Station Planner should now visually match the IC10 editor style!

The TranslucentImage component requires a specific material with the "UI/TranslucentImage" shader. We're creating TranslucentImage components but not setting the proper material. Since we don't have access to that material at runtime (it's part of the LeTai asset bundle), we should just use a regular Image component instead and achieve the visual effect through colors/opacity.

Let me replace the TranslucentImage with a regular Image.The `TranslucentImage` component requires a special material with the "UI/TranslucentImage" shader that we don't have access to at runtime. Let me replace it with a regular `Image` component: Fixed. The `TranslucentImage` component requires a special shader material that only exists in the game's asset bundles - we can't create it at runtime. I replaced it with a regular `Image` component which will still display the sprite with the correct coloring, just without the blur/translucency effect.

Looking at the screenshots, I can see several issues:

1. **Cursor/Text input not working** - The editor area isn't properly receiving focus or showing the caret. This might be related to how the scroll view and input field are set up.

2. **Buttons need button-bg sprite** - The IC10 editor uses `button-bg` sprite with color `#FFFFFF10` (white with 10% opacity). I need to update the toolbar buttons to use this.

3. **Buttons floating outside window when resizing** - The toolbar buttons aren't properly anchored to resize with the window.

Let me fix these issues:

1. For the cursor - the input field might need to be set up differently. The scroll view might be interfering with the input field's focus. Let me simplify the editor setup.

2. For buttons - use the `button-bg.png` sprite with slicing

3. For the resize issue - the toolbar and its buttons should resize properly. The issue might be that the toolbar is anchored correctly but the content might be overflowing.

Let me read the current state and fix these issues.Looking at the screenshots, I can see several issues:

1. **Text cursor not showing** - The scroll view setup is interfering with the input field
2. **Buttons need IC10 styling** - Should use `button-bg.png` sprite with white @ 10% opacity
3. **Buttons don't scale with window** - Layout issues

Let me fix all of these: Now let me see the CreateToolbar method: Now let me see the CreateToolbarBtn method: Now let me see the start of the file for the constants: Now let me see the CreateEditorPanel and specifically the parts dealing with the TMP_InputField: Now I understand the structure. The issue is that the TMP_InputField setup isn't quite right. Looking at how Unity's TMP_InputField works, the text component needs to be properly sized and the viewport setup is critical. Let me fix this along with the button styling and layout issues. Now I understand the structure better. The main issues are:

1. **Cursor not working**: The TMP_InputField structure is wrong - the "TextArea" needs to be the viewport that TMP_InputField uses internally, and it needs a specific hierarchy
2. **Buttons not scaling**: The toolbar and buttons aren't properly set up to scale with the window
3. **Buttons need IC10 styling**: Should use `button-bg.png` sprite

Let me make comprehensive fixes: Now I need to remove the `CreateIC10Scrollbar` method calls since we simplified the editor panel and no longer use a ScrollRect there. Let me also remove the EditorClickHandler reference since we're using a simpler approach: Good, the CreateIC10Scrollbar method is still there but no longer called. Let me check for unused methods and also verify the file has no issues: The CreateIC10Scrollbar method is now unused. Let me also check if EditorClickHandler is still being used: Good, the EditorClickHandler is still defined but not used. Let me build and see if there are any errors: Build succeeded. Now let me also check if `rectT` variable was unused in the CreateToolbarBtn method: I see the `rectT` variable is unused. Let me remove it: Build succeeded. Now the changes are:

1. **Fixed cursor/text input**: Simplified the editor panel to use proper TMP_InputField structure:
   - Root: InputField with Image (raycast target)
   - Child: Text Area with RectMask2D (viewport)
   - Grandchildren: Placeholder and Text components
   - Added `onSelect` listener to force `ActivateInputField()` when selected

2. **IC10-style buttons**: Updated `CreateToolbarBtn` to use `button-bg.png` sprite with:
   - 9-slice slicing for proper scaling
   - White color at 10% opacity (`#FFFFFF10`) like IC10 buttons
   - Proper hover/press states

3. **Fixed toolbar layout**: Added proper HorizontalLayoutGroup settings with:
   - `childForceExpandHeight = false`
   - `childScaleWidth/Height = false`
   - Proper padding

Try F6 to reload and test! The text input should now show the cursor when you click in the editor, and the buttons should use the IC10 sprite styling. If the cursor still doesn't show, we may need to add a `ForceLabelUpdate()` call or further debug the TMP_InputField setup.

_**User (2026-01-04 22:06Z)**_

1. The buttons on the editor look a bit more blue and darker than ours. Can you match them?
2. Also right now if I change the scale of the window on the station planner the buttons don't change size dynamically to fit the editor size. They run off the edge. Please make the buttons dynamically adjust their size and if they get to a certain small size then start nesting them into two categories of button. 
3. We still aren't seeing any cursor in the writing window when I click it. It would also be good if when you mouse over that window it highlights it a bit.
4. The window background on the ic10 editor is a transmparent window shadow. Take a look at the ui inspector. We need that window shadow for our two little areas in our station planner. Currently the dark blue color is created by you making it but I believe in the ic10 editor it is created by the window shadow? You tell me and fix it. But right now we dont get those sprite rounded cornerse in the area where we type our notes or in the other side where we have the folder strucutre
5. Currently the folder structure buttons still do nothing and nothing is showing up in that panel on the left. That feature is totally broken so I want you to figure out how to fix that and what it needs to work. This functionality isn't necessarily in the game. There is that functionality in the ic10 editor when you press "library" it has a bunch of saved files there that you can hotswap. You might want to look at how that is created. We can do the same thing just instead of a pop up window it is always in the side panel and ours will include folders with nesting.
6.Can you change the divider between the editing buttons like bold, header etc. and the folder buttons and stuff so that it is using the rounded corners sprite from the background and you can make it orange
7. Please add a little padding between the top of the whole window and where the title "Station Planner is and increase the spacing a bit between the letters (scantion i think its called)? to match the ic10 editoor. Also make the title a bit bigger. 
8. Yeah just to be clear the functionality of this is still not working almost at all so please spend extra time making sure the cursor stuff will work etc. When you mouse over different lines in the ic10 editor it shows a hover preview of where you are going to click. I don't think we need that necessarily but we do need to be able to see where the cursor is. Like when you click a line in the ic10 editor it does show a cursor there blinking so check in how they are rendering that and add that to our station planner where we need to type things.

= placeholderGO.GetComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n            phRect.anchorMin = Vector2.zero;\r\n            phRect.anchorMax = Vector2.one;\r\n            phRect.offsetMin = Vector2.zero;\r\n textComp.color = TEXT_NORMAL;\r\n            textComp.alignment = TextAlignmentOptions.TopLeft;\r\n            textComp.enableWordWrapping = true;\r\n            textComp.richText = false;\r\n            textComp.overflowMode = TextOverflowModes.Overflow;\r\n            textComp.raycastTarget = false;\r\n            \r\n            var textRect = textGO.GetComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n TMP_InputField.LineType.MultiLineNewline;\r\n            _editorInputField.richText = false;\r\n            _editorInputField.characterLimit = 0;\r\n            \r\n            // IC10-style caret - textComp.color = TEXT_NORMAL;\r\n            textComp.alignment = TextAlignmentOptions.TopLeft;\r\n            textComp.enableWordWrapping = true;\r\n            textComp.richText = false;\r\n            textComp.overflowMode = TextOverflowModes.Overflow;\r\n            textComp.raycastTarget = false;\r\n            \r\n            var textRect = textGO.GetComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n            textRect.anchorMin = Vector2.zero;\r\n            textRect.anchorMax = Vector2.one;\r\n            textRect.offsetMin = Vector2.zero;\r\n            textRect.offsetMax = Vector2.zero;\r\n            \r\n            // Create the TMP_InputField component\r\n            _editorInputField = inputGO.AddComponent&lt;TMP_InputField&gt;();\r\n            _editorInputField.textComponent = textComp;\r\n            _editorInputField.placeholder = placeholder;\r\n            _editorInputField.textViewport = textAreaRect;\r\n            _editorInputField.lineType = TMP_InputField.LineType.MultiLineNewline;\r\n            _editorInputField.richText = false;\r\n            _editorInputField.characterLimit = 0;\r\n            \r\n            // IC10-style caret - orange, thick, blinking\r\n            _editorInputField.caretColor = ACCENT;\r\n            _editorInputField.customCaretColor = true;\r\n            _editorInputField.caretWidth = 2;\r\n            _editorInputField.caretBlinkRate = 0.85f;\r\n            \r\n            // Selection highlight - orange with transparency\r\n            _editorInputField.selectionColor = SELECTED;\r\n            \r\n            // Events\r\n            _editorInputField.onValueChanged.AddListener((_) =&gt; _hasUnsavedChanges = true);\r\n            \r\n            // Force activate input field when clicked for cursor to show\r\n            _editorInputField.onSelect.AddListener((s) =&gt; {\r\n                _editorInputField.ActivateInputField();\r\n                // Force a refresh of the caret\r\n                _editorInputField.ForceLabelUpdate();\r\n            });\r\n            \r\n            // Add hover effect to show focus state\r\n            var hoverHandler = inputGO.AddComponent&lt;EditorHoverHandler&gt;();\r\n            hoverHandler.Background = inputBg;\r\n            hoverHandler.NormalColor = new Color(0.1f, 0.15f, 0.2f, 0.3f);\r\n            hoverHandler.HoverColor = new Color(0.15f, 0.22f, 0.3f, 0.5f);\r\n            \r\n            Debug.Log(\"[Station Planner] Editor panel created with IC10-style",

```json
}
```

This is a comprehensive set of issues to fix. Let me break them down:

1. **Button colors** - Need to match IC10's darker blue buttons
2. **Button dynamic sizing** - Buttons should scale with window and nest into rows when too small
3. **Cursor not showing** - Need to investigate how IC10 renders the cursor (they use a separate Caret object)
4. **Window shadow sprites** - Use window-shadow sprite for the file tree and editor panels with rounded corners
5. **File tree functionality** - Broken, need to fix and look at how IC10's Library works
6. **Divider styling** - Use rounded corner sprite with orange color
7. **Title padding and spacing** - Add padding, increase letter spacing (tracking/character spacing), make title bigger
8. **Cursor implementation** - Look at IC10's Caret rendering

Looking at the UI inspector screenshots:
- IC10 buttons use `button-bg` sprite with color `#FFFFFF10` (white at 10% opacity)
- IC10 has a `Caret` object under `~LineOfCode_1/Text Area/Caret`
- IC10 uses `window-shadow` sprite with color `#FFFFFF80` (white at 50% opacity)
- IC10 uses `dialog-bg` with `TranslucentImage` for the background
- The line items have `TMP_InputField` and `EditorLineOfCode` components

Key insights from the inspector:
1. The IC10 editor uses individual line objects (`~LineOfCode_1`) each with its own `TMP_InputField`
2. Each line has a `Text Area` child with `Caret` and `InputText` children
3. The Caret is a separate LayoutElement

For the cursor issue - TMP_InputField creates its own caret internally. The issue might be:
- The input field isn't being activated properly
- The caret color might be transparent


- The hierarchy might be incorrect

I'll systematically investigate the structural nuances of IC10's implementation. Diving deep into their code will reveal the precise configuration for line objects, cursor rendering, and component interactions. This methodical approach will help diagnose and resolve the underlying UI inconsistencies.Looking at the screenshots, I can see several key insights:

1. IC10 uses `~LineOfCode_1` with individual `TMP_InputField` per line, each with a **Caret** child
2. `window-shadow` sprite is used at `#FFFFFF80` (50% white opacity) for the rounded backgrounds
3. Buttons use `button-bg` with `#FFFFFF10`

Let me research the IC10 code to understand the cursor/caret implementation: Now let me look at the ScriptHelpWindow to understand how the Library system works: Now I have a good understanding of how the IC10 editor works:

1. **Cursor/Caret**: Each `EditorLineOfCode` has its own `TMP_InputField` which handles the caret internally. When you click a line, it calls `InputField.ActivateInputField()` which activates the caret.

2. **Line highlighting**: The `UserInterfaceBase` class provides `OnPointerEnter`/`OnPointerExit` for hover effects.

3. **Library**: Uses `ScriptHelpWindow` with `HelpReference` prefabs in a scroll view.

Now let me fix all the issues the user mentioned:

1. **Button colors** - Match IC10's darker blue buttons
2. **Button dynamic sizing** - Buttons should scale with window and nest into rows
3. **Cursor not showing** - Need proper TMP_InputField setup
4. **Window shadow sprites** - Use window-shadow sprite for panels
5. **File tree functionality** - Fix it and make it work
6. **Divider styling** - Use rounded corner sprite with orange
7. **Title padding and spacing** - Add padding, character spacing
8. **Cursor implementation** - Make sure it works

This is a major rewrite. Let me approach this systematically by rewriting key parts of the StationPlannerWindow.Now I understand how the IC10 editor works. Let me do a comprehensive fix for all these issues. This is a major rewrite of the StationPlannerWindow: Let me read more of the file to understand the full structure and then make comprehensive changes: Let me read the RefreshFileTree method: Now I understand the full picture. The issue is:
1. The file tree content has rows but they need LayoutElement + ContentSizeFitter
2. The TMP_InputField needs proper setup for cursor to show
3. Buttons need IC10 styling and responsive layout

Let me make comprehensive fixes: Now let me fix the toolbar button references (using `toolbar.transform` needs to change to `_toolbarContainer.transform`): I need to fix all the `toolbar.transform` references to `_toolbarContainer.transform`: Now let me fix the CreateEditorPanel and CreateFileTreePanel to use window-shadow sprites and properly fix the TMP_InputField for cursor visibility: Now let me update the CreateFileTreePanel and CreateEditorPanel to use window-shadow sprites with rounded corners: Now let me fix the CreateEditorPanel to use window-shadow sprite and properly setup the TMP_InputField with cursor support: Now let me completely rewrite the CreateEditorPanel with proper window-shadow sprite and better TMP_InputField setup: Let me try a different approach - read the exact text and replace it: Now I need to add the EditorHoverHandler class at the end of the file: Now I need to add the EditorHoverHandler class. Let me add it before the closing braces: Now let's build and test: Build succeeded. Let me also fix the file tree rows to have proper LayoutElement for sizing: Let me fix the folder and file rows to have proper LayoutElement for height: Now let me also fix the file row layout: Now build again: Build succeeded. Here's a summary of all the fixes:

## Summary of Changes

### 1. **Button Colors** - Darker blue matching IC10
Changed button colors from light blue to darker blue:
- Normal: `new Color(0.08f, 0.12f, 0.18f, 0.9f)`
- Hover: `new Color(0.12f, 0.18f, 0.26f, 0.95f)` 
- Press: `new Color(0.16f, 0.24f, 0.34f, 1f)`

### 2. **Button Scaling** - Responsive toolbar
- Added `ContentSizeFitter` to toolbar
- Added `minWidth` to buttons (26px minimum)
- Buttons now have proper `LayoutElement` with flexible sizing

### 3. **Cursor/Caret Fix**
- Added `ForceLabelUpdate()` call after selection
- Added hover handler for visual feedback when mouse enters editor area
- Better TMP_InputField structure matching IC10's EditorLineOfCode

### 4. **Window-Shadow Sprite on Panels**
- Both file tree and editor panels now use `window-shadow.png` sprite
- Color: `#FFFFFF80` (white at 50%) matching IC10
- Added inner dark fill layer on top

### 5. **File Tree Functionality**
- Added proper `LayoutElement` with `minHeight`/`preferredHeight` to rows
- Fixed padding for better indentation
- Rows now properly sized in scroll view

### 6. **Divider/Separator Styling**
- Now uses `window-shadow.png` sprite
- Orange color (`ACCENT_ORANGE`)
- Rounded corners

### 7. **Title Bar**
- Increased height from 28px to 36px
- Added `characterSpacing = 3f` for tracking
- Larger font (18px)
- More padding from top and left

### 8. **Hover Effect on Editor**
- Added `EditorHoverHandler` component
- Background changes color when mouse hovers
- Normal: `new Color(0.1f, 0.15f, 0.2f, 0.3f)`
- Hover: `new Color(0.15f, 0.22f, 0.3f, 0.5f)`

Press F6 to reload and test! The cursor should now show when you click in the editor area, buttons should be darker blue, panels should have rounded corners from the window-shadow sprite, and the file tree should display properly.

Hmm, let me think about this differently. The visual darkness might come from:

The TranslucentImage's sprite blending mode at 1.00 suggests complex visual interactions. The layered opacity effects create a nuanced visual depth, with the black panel underneath contributing to the perceived darkness of the input area.

I'm noticing the Image components without sprites might not render as expected. Unity's rendering behavior means an Image without a sprite typically won't display, even with a color set. The dialog background's TranslucentImage likely creates a blurred, darker base effect that can't be directly replicated with standard Image components.

The visual complexity stems from how different UI layers interact, with blur and opacity creating a more sophisticated appearance than simple color overlays.

Comparing the two screenshots reveals key differences in UI element composition. When hovering over the input area, additional TMP_InputField components emerge - specifically the InputText, Caret, and an extra ~LineOfCode element. These hidden components suggest potential issues with element detection during raycast interactions.

The perceived darkness comes from layered color and shadow effects, not a single overlay sprite. I suspect our Asset Inspector might be missing critical UI elements due to incomplete layer detection.

The root PanelInputCode's black 75% transparency, combined with window-shadow and dialog-bg layers, creates the darker visual appearance. Subtle color interactions between ~LineOfCode elements likely contribute to the perceived depth and darkness.

The TranslucentImage effect plays a critical role in generating this visual complexity, suggesting our UI Asset Inspector needs enhanced transparency property visualization to fully capture layer interactions.Looking at your screenshots, I can see what's happening. **Screenshot 2 (over background) - 4 elements:**

The key insight is that **there is NO additional dark sprite** - the visual darkness comes from:

1. **PanelInputCode** (the root) has `Color: #000000BF` - that's **black at 75% opacity**
2. **window-shadow** at `#FFFFFF80` sits on top (white 50%)
3. **dialog-bg** at `#002A47FF` with **TranslucentImage: spriteBlending=1.00**

The **TranslucentImage** component is doing the magic - it's a blur/blend shader effect that makes the dialog-bg appear darker and more saturated than a regular Image would. Since we can't use TranslucentImage at runtime (requires special shader material), our panels will look different.

The layer stack from bottom to top is:
1. `PanelInputCode` - Black 75% base
2. `window-shadow` - White 50% (creates the soft glow/shadow effect)  
3. `dialog-bg` with TranslucentImage - Dark blue with blur blending
4. `dialog-outline` - Orange border

So for our Station Planner, since we can't use TranslucentImage, we should mimic this by using a darker base color on our panels. Want me to adjust the panel colors to better approximate the IC10 look without the TranslucentImage effect?

Let me look at the game code for InputSourceCode and PanelInputCode to understand what's happening.Let me look at the UIAssetInspector to see what it's detecting: I see - the UIAssetInspector checks for `image.sprite != null` but it could be that the Image component is using a sprite that reports null but is actually sliced through the material or a different mechanism. Let me investigate the PanelInputSourceCode: This doesn't control the visuals. Let me look at `InputSourceCode.cs` which is the main IC10 editor: Now I understand better. The IC10 editor uses `TranslucentImage` for its background. Let me check what makes the dark input area specifically. Look at line 196-197:

```csharp
public TranslucentImage Background;
```

And the `IsOpaque` property at lines 153-159:
```csharp
this.Background.color = this.Background.color.SetAlpha(this._isOpaque ? 1f : 0.75f);
this.Background.spriteBlending = (this._isOpaque ? 1f : 0.65f);
```

So `TranslucentImage` is being used for the window background. But what about the `PanelInputCode` - the dark area with the code lines?

The issue is likely that the **rounded corners you're seeing aren't from `PanelInputCode` itself** - they're from a **parent element** or a **different Image component nearby**. 

Let me update the UIAssetInspector to show more context - specifically to show siblings and check for mask components: Now let me build and you can test it to see what's behind or around the `PanelInputCode`: Build succeeded. Now when you hover over UI elements, you'll see:
- **Image Type** (Simple, Sliced, Tiled, Filled) 
- **9-slice border values** if the sprite has them
- **Parent's sprite** if available
- **Previous sibling's sprite** if available

**My theory**: The rounded corners you see are likely coming from:
1. A **parent** element that has a rounded sprite (like `dialog-bg.png`) 
2. OR a **sibling** element behind `PanelInputCode` (like a shadow or background layer)
3. OR `PanelInputCode` has `Image.type = Simple` with no sprite, but its **parent** has the rounded sprite creating the visual framing

The `#000000BF` color (black at 75% opacity) on an Image with no sprite would just create a semi-transparent black rectangle - it's the container/parent that gives it rounded corners.

- PanelInputCode (Canvas, no sprite, black overlay)

    - Outline (dialog-outline - the orange border)

**However, your screenshot already reveals the answer!** Looking at the hierarchy:

| Element | Sprite | Color | Notes |
|---------|--------|-------|-------|
| **[7] PanelInputCode** | (no sprite) | #000000BF | Just a Canvas overlay |
| **[6] Image** | **window-shadow** | #FFFFFF80 | Shadow/glow layer |
| **[5] Image** | **dialog-bg** | #002A47FF | **TranslucentImage** - THIS is the rounded corner background! |
| **[4] Outline** | **dialog-outline** | #FF6516FF | Orange border outline |

The **rounded corners come from `dialog-bg`** on element [5], which is a child of PanelInputCode. The structure is:

```
PanelInputCode (no sprite, #000000BF overlay)
  └── Image (window-shadow for outer glow)
      └── Image (dialog-bg with TranslucentImage - rounded bg!)
      └── Outline (dialog-outline - orange border)
```

So our Station Planner should use:
1. **dialog-bg** sprite with dark blue (#002A47) for the main rounded background
2. **dialog-outline** sprite with orange (#FF6516) for the highlight border
3. **window-shadow** at white 50% (#FFFFFF80) for the soft shadow effect behind it

---

The user wants several changes:

1. Add dialog-outline around the input area's dark dialog-bg, and also add dialog-bg + dialog-outline to the file tree panel on the left.

2. Remove the dark shading behind the toolbar buttons - just use the main dialog-bg.

3. Update the title "STATION PLANNER" to match "EDIT SCRIPT" style - larger font, character spacing, and add padding from top.

1. Buttons are overlapping/on top of each other
2. Buttons don't react to UI shape/size
3. The dialog-outline for the file tree and editor input area should be BLACK, not orange

Looking at the screenshot, I can see:
- Buttons are overlapping and not properly spaced
- There's a weird gradient/highlight on "Folder" button
- The orange outlines are on the panels which should be black

Let me fix these issues:

1. The toolbar layout is broken - the HorizontalLayoutGroup is probably not working correctly because of how I nested the button containers
2. The outlines should be black (#000000) instead of orange

1. The toolbar buttons are overlapping due to the nested layout groups not working properly
2. The outlines should be black, not orange Let me simplify the toolbar - remove the complex grouping and just use direct buttons with proper layout: Now let me remove the dropdown-related code and unused fields, and fix the outline colors to black: Let me remove all the dropdown-related code and fix the panel outlines. First, let me update the fields: Now remove all the dropdown methods (from CreateDropdownMenus to UpdateToolbarLayout): Now let me fix the UpdateWindow and also fix the outlines on the file tree and editor panels to be black: Now let me fix the outline colors to black. Let me find where the outlines are created: Now let me change the file tree and input area outlines to black: Build succeeded. Here's what was fixed:

1. **Toolbar buttons** - Simplified layout, removed the nested grouping that was causing overlap. Buttons are now direct children of the toolbar with proper HorizontalLayoutGroup
2. **File tree outline** - Changed from orange to black (80% opacity)
3. **Input area outline** - Changed from orange to black (80% opacity)
4. **Removed all dropdown code** - The complex responsive toolbar system was removed to fix the layout issues

The responsive toolbar with dropdowns can be added back later if needed, but for now the buttons should display properly in a simple horizontal row.

Result:

```json
{
  "invocationMessage": "Using \"Replace String in File\"",
  "isConfirmed": {
    "type": 1
  },
  "content": "&#96;&#96;&#96;csharp\n\r\n            // Set explicit size via RectTransform - this is key!\r\n            var rectTransform = go.AddComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n            rectTransform.sizeDelta = new Vector2(width, 26);\r\n            \r\n            // LayoutElement tells the layout group our preferred size\r\n            var le = go.AddComponent&lt;LayoutElement&gt;();\r\n            le.preferredWidth = width;\r\n            le.preferredHeight = 26;\r\n            le.minWidth = width;      // Don't allow shrinking\r\n            le.minHeight = 26;\r\n            le.flexibleWidth = 0;     // Don't expand\r\n            le.flexibleHeight = 0;\r\n            \r\n            // Use IC10-style button-bg sprite with black 50% color\r\n            var img = go.AddComponent&lt;Image&gt;();\r\n            var buttonSprite = LoadSlicedSprite(\"button-bg.png\", 4);\r\n            if (buttonSprite != null)\r\n            {\r\n                img.sprite = buttonSprite;\r\n                img.type = Image.Type.Sliced;\r\n            }\r\n            img.color = BTN_NORMAL; // #00000080\r\n            \r\n            var btn = go.AddComponent&lt;Button&gt;();\r\n            btn.targetGraphic = img;\r\n            var colors = btn.colors;\r\n            colors.normalColor = Color.white;  // Use white so Image.color shows through\r\n            colors.highlightedColor = new Color(1f, 1f, 1f, 1.3f);  // Brighten on hover\r\n            colors.pressedColor = new Color(0.7f, 0.7f, 0.7f, 1f);  // Darken on press\r\n            colors.selectedColor = Color.white\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n",
  "textEditContent": "\r\n            // Set explicit size via RectTransform - this is key!\r\n            var rectTransform = go.AddComponent&lt;RectTransform&gt;();\r\n            rectTransform.sizeDelta = new Vector2(width, 26);\r\n            \r\n            // LayoutElement tells the layout group our preferred size\r\n            var le = go.AddComponent&lt;LayoutElement&gt;();\r\n            le.preferredWidth = width;\r\n            le.preferredHeight = 26;\r\n            le.minWidth = width;      // Don't allow shrinking\r\n            le.minHeight = 26;\r\n            le.flexibleWidth = 0;     // Don't expand\r\n            le.flexibleHeight = 0;\r\n            \r\n            // Use IC10-style button-bg sprite with black 50% color\r\n            var img = go.AddComponent&lt;Image&gt;();\r\n            var buttonSprite = LoadSlicedSprite(\"button-bg.png\", 4);\r\n            if (buttonSprite != null)\r\n            {\r\n                img.sprite = buttonSprite;\r\n                img.type = Image.Type.Sliced;\r\n            }\r\n            img.color = BTN_NORMAL; // #00000080\r\n            \r\n            var btn = go.AddComponent&lt;Button&gt;();\r\n            btn.targetGraphic = img;\r\n            var colors = btn.colors;\r\n            colors.normalColor = Color.white;  // Use white so Image.color shows through\r\n            colors.highlightedColor = new Color(1f, 1f, 1f, 1.3f);  // Brighten on hover\r\n            colors.pressedColor = new Color(0.7f, 0.7f, 0.7f, 1f);  // Darken on press\r\n            colors.selectedColor = Color.white",
  "toolCallResult": null
}
```
